content.explanation:
The most fundamental data fetching pattern in React uses the useEffect hook to trigger a fetch call when a component mounts or when dependencies change. Inside useEffect, you call fetch (or any async operation), parse the response, and store the result in state. Alongside the data, you maintain loading and error states to give users feedback while the request is in flight.

The pattern follows a clear structure: (1) declare state for data, loading, and error; (2) inside useEffect, set loading to true, perform the fetch, update data on success or error on failure, and set loading to false in both cases; (3) render conditionally based on these states. This gives you a complete lifecycle for any async operation.

A critical detail is the cleanup function. When the component unmounts before the fetch completes, or when the dependency changes mid-flight, you need to ignore the stale response. The classic approach is to use a boolean flag (let ignore = false) and set it to true in the cleanup. Before updating state, check if ignore is true. The AbortController API is even better — it actually cancels the HTTP request, saving bandwidth.

Race conditions are a common bug in this pattern. If a user rapidly switches between pages or filters, multiple fetches launch in parallel and responses can arrive out of order. The last-dispatched fetch might resolve before an earlier one, causing stale data to overwrite fresh data. Using an AbortController or the ignore-flag pattern prevents this.

While this pattern works and is important to understand, modern React apps prefer React Query or SWR for production data fetching. These libraries handle caching, deduplication, retries, and race conditions automatically. However, understanding the manual useEffect pattern is essential for interviews and for understanding what those libraries abstract away.

codeExamples.title: Basic fetch with loading and error states
codeExamples.output: Shows 'Loading...' then user's name, or 'Error: ...' on failure
codeExamples.explanation: The ignore flag prevents state updates after unmount or when userId changes. Without it, a stale response could update state for the wrong userId.

codeExamples.title: Using AbortController for cancellation
codeExamples.output: Cancels previous request when query changes, shows latest results
codeExamples.explanation: AbortController.abort() cancels the in-flight request entirely. This saves bandwidth and prevents race conditions. The AbortError is caught and ignored since it is expected.

codeExamples.title: Custom useFetch hook
codeExamples.output: Reusable hook that handles loading, error, and cancellation
codeExamples.explanation: Extracting the pattern into a custom hook reduces duplication. However, this is essentially a simplified version of what React Query provides with far more features.

notes: Always handle the loading, success, and error states — never leave the user staring at a blank screen.
Use AbortController for cleanup to actually cancel the network request, not just ignore the response.
This manual pattern is great for learning but in production, use React Query or SWR which handle caching, retries, and deduplication automatically.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting the cleanup function in useEffect, causing 'can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component' warnings and race conditions.
Using async/await directly in useEffect (useEffect(() => async ...) is not allowed). Instead, define an async function inside and call it immediately.

exercises.title: Simulated Fetch with States
exercises.description: Simulate the useEffect fetch pattern without React. Create a function that takes a URL, simulates loading, then returns data. Track and log the loading, success, and error states throughout the lifecycle.
exercises.expectedOutput:
--- Success ---
Loading: true, Data: null, Error: null
Loading: false, Data: Response from /api/users, Error: null
--- Error ---
Loading: true, Data: null, Error: null
Loading: false, Data: null, Error: Network error
hints: Track state as an object with data, loading, and error fields. Spread to create new state objects.
The shouldFail parameter determines whether to simulate a success or error response.

exercises.title: Race Condition Prevention
exercises.description: Demonstrate how an ignore flag prevents race conditions. Simulate two fetch calls where the first one is slower than the second. Without the ignore flag, stale data overwrites fresh data. With it, the stale response is discarded.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Without ignore flag:
After B resolves: Fresh data from Request B
After A resolves: Stale data from Request A
Bug! Stale data displayed
---
With ignore flag:
After B resolves: Fresh data from Request B
Request A ignored (stale)
Final data: Fresh data from Request B
hints: In the broken version, both requests update currentData and the slower one wins. In the fixed version, the ignore flag prevents the stale response from updating state.
The ignore flag is set to true when the dependency changes (simulating the cleanup function in useEffect).

content.explanation:
Axios is an HTTP client library that improves on the native fetch API with features like automatic JSON parsing, request/response interceptors, timeout support, and better error handling. While fetch is built into browsers, Axios provides a more ergonomic API and is especially useful in production applications that need consistent error handling and authentication headers.

The recommended pattern is to create an Axios instance with a base URL and default configuration, then export it for use throughout the app. This centralises your API configuration in one place. If your API base URL or default headers change, you update one file instead of every fetch call.

Interceptors are the most powerful feature of Axios. A request interceptor runs before every request — the most common use is attaching the Authorization header with a JWT token. A response interceptor runs after every response — you can use it to handle 401 errors globally (redirect to login), transform response data, or log errors. This eliminates repetitive error handling in every component.

Error handling in Axios is more structured than fetch. Fetch only rejects on network errors, not on HTTP error codes (404, 500 still resolve). Axios rejects on any non-2xx status code, making it easier to catch errors in .catch() or try/catch. The error object includes response (server responded with error), request (no response received), and message (setup error).

A typical production setup creates an Axios instance, adds a request interceptor for auth tokens, adds a response interceptor for global error handling (401 redirect, toast notifications), and exports the instance. Components and React Query queryFn functions import this instance and use it for all API calls.

codeExamples.title: Creating a configured Axios instance
codeExamples.output: All requests go to https://api.example.com/v1/... with 10s timeout
codeExamples.explanation: axios.create returns a new instance with the given defaults. All requests made through this instance inherit the baseURL, timeout, and headers.

codeExamples.title: Request and response interceptors
codeExamples.explanation: The request interceptor reads the token from storage and injects it into every request. The response interceptor handles 401 (redirect to login) and 500 (log error) globally, so individual components don't need to.

codeExamples.title: Axios error handling structure
codeExamples.explanation: Axios errors have three categories: response errors (server replied with error code), request errors (network failure), and setup errors (bad config). This structure lets you handle each case appropriately.

notes: Always create a shared Axios instance instead of using axios directly — it centralises configuration and makes interceptors work consistently.
Axios automatically parses JSON responses; you don't need to call .json() like with fetch.
Use interceptors for cross-cutting concerns (auth, logging, error handling) instead of duplicating logic in every API call.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting that Axios throws on non-2xx responses (unlike fetch which resolves) — make sure your catch blocks handle these.
Creating multiple Axios instances when only one is needed, causing interceptors to be applied inconsistently across the app.

exercises.title: Simulated API Client with Interceptors
exercises.description: Build a simplified API client that supports interceptors. Implement request interceptors (to add auth headers) and response interceptors (to handle errors). Log each step to show the interceptor pipeline.
hints: Store interceptors in arrays. Before the request, run all request interceptors on the config object. After the response, run all response interceptors.
Each interceptor is a function that receives the config/response and returns a modified version.

exercises.title: Error Classification
exercises.description: Simulate Axios-style error classification. Given different error scenarios (server error, network error, setup error), classify and log each error type with its details.
exercises.expectedOutput:
--- Server Error ---
Type: Response Error
Status: 404
Message: Not Found
--- Network Error ---
Type: Request Error
Message: No response received
--- Setup Error ---
Type: Setup Error
Message: Invalid URL
hints: Check for error.response first (server responded), then error.request (no response), then fall through to setup error.
This mirrors the exact error structure Axios uses — the order of checks matters.

content.explanation:
Beyond basic queries, React Query provides powerful tools for mutations (write operations), optimistic updates (instant UI feedback), and infinite queries (pagination/infinite scroll). These patterns handle the complex server-state scenarios that would require hundreds of lines of manual code.

useMutation is the write counterpart to useQuery. It handles POST, PUT, DELETE operations and provides onMutate, onSuccess, onError, and onSettled callbacks. The key pattern is to invalidate related queries after a successful mutation so the UI shows fresh data. For example, after creating a new todo, you invalidate the ['todos'] query to trigger a refetch.

Optimistic updates take this further. Instead of waiting for the server to confirm a mutation, you immediately update the UI to reflect the expected result. In onMutate, you cancel outgoing queries, snapshot the current data, and manually set the cache to the optimistic value. If the server returns an error, onError rolls back to the snapshot. This gives the user instant feedback, making the app feel faster.

Infinite queries with useInfiniteQuery handle paginated or cursor-based data. You provide a getNextPageParam function that extracts the next cursor/page from the last response. The hook returns pages (an array of page results), fetchNextPage, hasNextPage, and isFetchingNextPage. This maps directly to infinite scroll UIs or 'Load More' buttons.

Prefetching is another advanced technique. queryClient.prefetchQuery lets you start fetching data before the user navigates to a page (e.g., on hover over a link). When they actually navigate, the data is already in cache and the page renders instantly. Combined with staleTime, this creates a near-instant navigation experience.

codeExamples.title: useMutation with query invalidation
codeExamples.output: Button shows 'Adding...' during mutation, then todos list refreshes
codeExamples.explanation: invalidateQueries marks the ['todos'] cache as stale and triggers a refetch. The list updates with the new todo from the server.

codeExamples.title: Optimistic update pattern
codeExamples.explanation: onMutate saves a snapshot and updates the cache optimistically. If the server rejects the mutation, onError rolls back. onSettled always refetches to sync with server truth.

codeExamples.title: Infinite query for pagination
codeExamples.output: Shows posts with a 'Load More' button that fetches next pages
codeExamples.explanation: getNextPageParam extracts the next page number from the server response. data.pages is an array of all fetched pages. hasNextPage is true as long as getNextPageParam returns a value.

notes: Always invalidate related queries after mutations to keep the UI in sync with the server.
Optimistic updates improve perceived performance but add complexity — use them for frequent user actions like toggling, liking, or reordering.
Use useInfiniteQuery for any list that loads incrementally (infinite scroll, 'Load More' button, cursor-based pagination).
commonPitfalls: Forgetting to call invalidateQueries after a mutation, leaving the UI showing stale data until the user manually refreshes.
Not handling the rollback in onError for optimistic updates — if the server fails, the UI will show incorrect data permanently.

exercises.title: Mutation with Cache Update
exercises.description: Simulate a mutation that adds an item to a cached list. After the mutation, invalidate the cache and refetch. Log each step: the initial cache, the mutation, the invalidation, and the refetched data.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Fetched todos: ["Buy milk","Walk dog"]
Mutated: added "Read book" to server
Invalidated: todos
Fetched todos: ["Buy milk","Walk dog","Read book"]
hints: Keep separate 'cache' and 'serverData' objects. query reads from cache (or fetches from server). mutate writes to server. invalidate deletes the cache entry.
After invalidation, the next query call should see a cache miss and refetch from the (now updated) server data.

exercises.title: Optimistic Update with Rollback
exercises.description: Simulate an optimistic update. Update the cache immediately before the server confirms. If the server returns an error, roll back to the previous state. Log the cache state at each step.
exercises.expectedOutput:
=== Success Case ===
Initial cache: ["Todo 1","Todo 2"]
After optimistic update: ["Todo 1","Todo 2","Todo 3 (optimistic)"]
Server confirmed!
After confirmation: ["Todo 1","Todo 2","Todo 3 (confirmed)"]
=== Failure Case ===
Initial cache: ["Todo 1","Todo 2"]
After optimistic update: ["Todo 1","Todo 2","Todo 3 (optimistic)"]
Server error! Rolling back...
After rollback: ["Todo 1","Todo 2"]
hints: Take a snapshot (copy) of the cache BEFORE the optimistic update. This snapshot is your rollback target.
On success, replace the optimistic entry with the server-confirmed version. On failure, restore the snapshot.

content.explanation:
Error boundaries are React components that catch JavaScript errors anywhere in their child component tree, log those errors, and display a fallback UI instead of crashing the entire application. Without error boundaries, a single thrown error in any component will unmount the whole React tree, leaving users with a blank white screen.

Error boundaries must be class components — there is no hook equivalent for componentDidCatch and getDerivedStateFromError as of React 18. getDerivedStateFromError is a static method that runs during rendering and returns new state (typically { hasError: true }). componentDidCatch is an instance method that runs after the error has been committed — use it for logging to an error reporting service.

You should place error boundaries strategically. A top-level boundary around your entire app prevents the white screen of death. More granular boundaries around individual routes, widgets, or feature sections let the rest of the app continue working when one section fails. For example, if the chat widget throws, the main content should still be usable.

Error boundaries catch errors during rendering, in lifecycle methods, and in constructors of the whole tree below them. They do NOT catch errors in event handlers (use try/catch), asynchronous code (promises, setTimeout), server-side rendering, or errors thrown in the error boundary itself. For event handler errors, you need explicit try/catch blocks.

Recovery is an important UX concern. A good fallback UI includes a message explaining something went wrong and a 'Try Again' button that resets the error boundary state. You can do this by changing the key prop on the boundary (forcing a remount) or by providing a reset method via a render prop or callback.

codeExamples.title: Basic error boundary class component
codeExamples.output: If RiskyComponent throws, shows 'Something went wrong' with Try Again button
codeExamples.explanation: getDerivedStateFromError sets the error state during render. componentDidCatch logs the error after commit. The fallback UI includes a reset button.

codeExamples.title: Granular error boundaries per route
codeExamples.explanation: The outer boundary catches catastrophic errors. Inner boundaries per route let the header and navigation keep working even if a page crashes.

codeExamples.title: Error boundary with reset via key
codeExamples.output: Clicking 'Reset & Try Again' remounts the error boundary and its children
codeExamples.explanation: Changing the key forces React to unmount and remount the ErrorBoundary, clearing its error state and giving the children a fresh start.

notes: Error boundaries must be class components — there is no hooks API for catching render errors (as of React 18).
Place error boundaries at both the app level (prevent white screen) and at feature/route level (isolate failures).
Error boundaries do NOT catch errors in event handlers, async code, or SSR — use try/catch for those.
commonPitfalls: Assuming error boundaries catch event handler errors — they don't. A click handler that throws will not be caught; you need explicit try/catch inside the handler.
Placing only one error boundary at the root, which means any error still takes down the whole visible UI inside that boundary. Use granular boundaries.
interviewTips: Explain that error boundaries are the React equivalent of try/catch for the component tree — they prevent a single failing component from crashing the entire app.
Mention that they must be class components and explain what getDerivedStateFromError (sync, during render) and componentDidCatch (async, after commit) each do.
Discuss granular placement: root boundary for catastrophic errors, route-level boundaries for page isolation, widget-level boundaries for non-critical features.
Know the limitations: event handlers, async code, and SSR errors are NOT caught — this is a common follow-up question.

exercises.title: Simulated Error Boundary
exercises.description: Simulate an error boundary pattern without React. Create a function that wraps another function call, catches any thrown errors, and returns a fallback value. Log whether the call succeeded or was caught.
exercises.expectedOutput:
--- Success Case ---
Render success: Dashboard Content
--- Error Case ---
Error caught: Component crashed
Showing fallback: Something went wrong
--- Another Error ---
Error caught: Data is undefined
Showing fallback: Failed to load widget
hints: Use a try/catch block around the function call. Return the result on success, the fallback on error.
Log the error message from the caught error and the fallback value being displayed.

exercises.title: Nested Error Boundaries with Isolation
exercises.description: Simulate nested error boundaries. An outer boundary wraps the whole app. Inner boundaries wrap individual sections. If one section throws, the outer boundary should NOT catch it — only the inner boundary should. Show that other sections keep working.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Header: rendered OK
[Dashboard] caught: Dashboard crashed
Dashboard: Dashboard fallback UI
Settings: Settings Content
Result: App rendered
hints: Each errorBoundary function wraps a section. The inner boundary catches Dashboard's error, so the outer boundary never sees it.
The key insight is that inner boundaries catch errors first, preventing them from propagating to outer boundaries.

content.explanation:
React Suspense is a mechanism that lets components 'wait' for something before rendering — whether that is lazily loaded code, data, or other asynchronous resources. When a component inside a Suspense boundary is not ready, React shows the fallback UI (typically a loading spinner) until the component resolves.

The most established use of Suspense is with React.lazy for code splitting. React.lazy takes a function that returns a dynamic import and creates a component that loads the module on demand. Wrapping it with Suspense provides the loading state. This is the foundation of route-based code splitting — instead of loading your entire app's JavaScript upfront, each route's code loads only when the user navigates to it.

Route-based code splitting is the most impactful optimization because routes represent natural boundaries where users expect a brief loading moment. You lazy-load each page component and wrap your routes in a Suspense boundary. The initial bundle becomes much smaller, and subsequent routes load on demand. This dramatically improves Time to Interactive for large applications.

Suspense for data fetching is a newer pattern. Libraries like React Query (with its suspense option) and the experimental React.use hook let components suspend while data is loading. Instead of managing isLoading state manually, the component simply reads the data and Suspense handles the loading state. This leads to cleaner component code — no loading/error conditionals.

Suspense boundaries can be nested. A page-level boundary shows a full-page loader while the route loads. Inside that page, section-level boundaries can show inline spinners while individual data sources load. This gives you fine-grained control over the loading experience without cluttering components with loading logic.

codeExamples.title: React.lazy with Suspense for code splitting
codeExamples.output: Shows 'Loading page...' while route JS bundle downloads, then renders the page
codeExamples.explanation: Each route component is in a separate chunk. The browser only downloads Dashboard.js when the user navigates to /dashboard. Suspense shows the fallback during download.

codeExamples.title: Nested Suspense boundaries
codeExamples.output: Each widget shows its own loading state independently
codeExamples.explanation: Nested boundaries let each section load independently. The stats might appear first while the chart and activity feed are still loading. Users see progressive content instead of one big spinner.

codeExamples.title: Suspense with React Query
codeExamples.output: Shows ProfileSkeleton while loading, then renders user data
codeExamples.explanation: useSuspenseQuery suspends the component while data loads. The component code is cleaner — no isLoading check, data is always defined. The Suspense boundary handles the loading state.

notes: React.lazy only works with default exports. If your component uses named exports, create an intermediate module that re-exports it as default.
Place Suspense boundaries at natural loading points: route transitions, widget sections, modals that load content.
Suspense for data fetching requires library support (React Query's useSuspenseQuery, or experimental React.use). You cannot just throw a promise manually in production code.
commonPitfalls: Wrapping every single component in Suspense — this creates too many loading spinners and a janky UX. Use Suspense at meaningful boundaries (routes, page sections).
Using React.lazy inside a component body instead of at the module level. Defining it inside a component causes a new lazy component on every render, which resets state and re-triggers loading.

exercises.title: Simulated Lazy Loading
exercises.description: Simulate React.lazy behavior. Create a lazyLoad function that takes a loader (a function returning a value) and returns an object that starts 'loading' and transitions to 'loaded' with the value. Log the state at each step.
exercises.expectedOutput:
--- Loading Dashboard ---
Status: loading
Status: loaded
Value: Dashboard Component
--- Loading Settings ---
Status: loading
Status: loaded
Value: Settings Component
hints: Create an object with status: 'loading'. After calling the loader function, update status to 'loaded' and store the result.
This simulates what React.lazy does: it starts in a loading state and transitions to loaded once the dynamic import resolves.

exercises.title: Suspense Boundary Simulation
exercises.description: Simulate a Suspense boundary. Create a function that takes a render function and a fallback string. If the render function throws a 'SuspenseSignal', show the fallback. Otherwise, show the rendered content. Demonstrate with a component that suspends on first call and resolves on second.
exercises.expectedOutput:
--- First render (suspends) ---
Suspended! Showing fallback: Loading profile...
--- Second render (resolved) ---
Rendered: User: Alice
hints: Wrap the renderFn call in a try/catch. If the caught error has type 'SuspenseSignal', show the fallback instead of crashing.
The suspending component uses a closure variable (loaded) to throw on first call and return data on subsequent calls, simulating async data loading.
